annotations.label: Stochastic process (informal)

anchor.snippet: a stochastic ( / s t ə ˈ k æ s t ɪ k / ) or random process is a mathematical object usually defined as a family of random variables

annotations.note: Mathlib has no first-class `StochasticProcess` type; processes are represented informally as functions `ι → Ω → E` (used by `MeasureTheory.Filtration`, `Adapted`, `Martingale`, etc.).

annotations.label: Wiener and Poisson processes as classic examples

anchor.snippet: Two classic examples are the Wiener process

annotations.note: Searches for Wiener/Brownian/Poisson-process declarations in `Mathlib/Probability` found none (only the Poisson distribution `poissonPMF`).

annotations.label: Random field

anchor.snippet: then the collection of random variables is usually called a random field instead

annotations.note: No `RandomField` declaration exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Index set and state space

anchor.snippet: The set used to index the random variables is called the index set

annotations.note: No named definition of `index set`/`state space`; in Mathlib these appear only as implicit type parameters `ι` and `E` on process-shaped functions.

annotations.label: Increment (informal)

anchor.snippet: An increment is the amount that a stochastic process changes between two index values

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define a standalone `Increment`; it appears implicitly inside `HasIndepIncrements` as `X t' - X t`.

annotations.label: Sample function / realization (informal)

anchor.snippet: a single outcome of a stochastic process is called, among other names, a sample function or realization

annotations.note: No declaration corresponds to `sample function`; in Mathlib it is implicit as `fun t ↦ X t ω` for fixed `ω`.

annotations.label: Discrete-time vs continuous-time

anchor.snippet: then the stochastic process is said to be in discrete time

annotations.note: Mathlib makes no formal discrete-vs-continuous-time distinction; the index `ι` is a generic preorder/topological space.

annotations.label: Random sequence

anchor.snippet: then the stochastic process can also be called a random sequence

annotations.note: No `RandomSequence` declaration; sequences of random variables are simply `ℕ → Ω → E`.

annotations.label: Discrete / real-valued / vector process by state space

anchor.snippet: If the state space is the integers or natural numbers, then the stochastic process is called a discrete or integer-valued stochastic process

annotations.note: Mathlib does not classify processes by state space; this terminology is absent.

annotations.label: Bernoulli process

anchor.snippet: the Bernoulli process

annotations.note: Only the Bernoulli distribution / `setBernoulli` exist; no `BernoulliProcess` was found.

annotations.label: Random walk

anchor.snippet: Random walks are stochastic processes that are usually defined as sums of iid random variables or random vectors

annotations.note: Grep for `RandomWalk`/`random_walk` returns no hits in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simple random walk

anchor.snippet: A classic example of a random walk is known as the simple random walk

annotations.note: No simple-random-walk construction exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetric random walk

anchor.snippet: this random walk is called a symmetric random walk

annotations.note: No symmetric-random-walk declaration exists.

annotations.label: Wiener process

anchor.snippet: The Wiener process is a stochastic process with stationary and independent increments that are normally distributed based on the size of the increments

annotations.note: No Wiener/Brownian-motion declaration is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Drift of Brownian motion

anchor.snippet: then the resulting stochastic process is said to have drift

annotations.note: No `drift` notion for Brownian motion exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sample paths continuous nowhere differentiable

anchor.snippet: a sample path of a Wiener process is continuous everywhere but nowhere differentiable

annotations.note: Requires a formal Wiener process, which Mathlib does not have.

annotations.label: Donsker's theorem / functional CLT

anchor.snippet: which is the subject of Donsker's theorem or invariance principle, also known as the functional central limit theorem

annotations.note: Grep finds no Donsker/functional CLT/invariance-principle formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Poisson process (counting)

anchor.snippet: It can be defined as a counting process, which is a stochastic process that represents the random number of points or events up to some time

annotations.note: Only the Poisson distribution `poissonPMF` is formalized; no Poisson process or counting-process notion.

annotations.label: Homogeneous Poisson process

anchor.snippet: then the process is called a homogeneous Poisson process

annotations.note: No Poisson-process formalization exists, so neither does the homogeneous variant.

annotations.label: Stationary Poisson process

anchor.snippet: this stochastic process is also called the stationary Poisson process

annotations.note: No Poisson-process formalization exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inhomogeneous Poisson process

anchor.snippet: the resulting process is called an inhomogeneous or nonhomogeneous Poisson process

annotations.note: No inhomogeneous Poisson-process formalization exists.

annotations.label: Stochastic process (formal)

anchor.snippet: A stochastic process is defined as a collection of random variables defined on a common probability space

annotations.note: Stochastic processes are represented informally as functions `ι → Ω → E` (no dedicated structure); supporting machinery like `Filtration`, `Adapted`, `Martingale` is in `Mathlib.Probability.Process`.

annotations.label: Stochastic process as function-valued RV

anchor.snippet: a stochastic process can be interpreted or defined as a

annotations.note: The function-valued view appears via `P.map (fun ω ↦ (X · ω))` in `FiniteDimensionalLaws`, but there is no `StochasticProcess` type.

annotations.label: Index set / parameter set

anchor.snippet: is called the index set

annotations.note: No named `IndexSet`; the index appears only as a generic type parameter `T`/`ι`.

annotations.label: State space

anchor.snippet: of a stochastic process is called its state space

annotations.note: No named `StateSpace`; it is implicit as the codomain type `E` of the process.

annotations.label: Sample function / path

anchor.snippet: A sample function is a single outcome of a stochastic process

annotations.note: No `SampleFunction`/`SamplePath` definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Increment

anchor.snippet: An increment of a stochastic process is the difference between two random variables of the same stochastic process

annotations.note: Increments `X t - X s` appear inside `HasIndepIncrements` but have no standalone definition.

annotations.label: Law of a stochastic process

anchor.snippet: the law of stochastic process

annotations.note: The law of a process is `P.map (fun ω ↦ (X · ω))`, formalized via `isProjectiveLimit_map` in `FiniteDimensionalLaws`.

annotations.label: Finite-dimensional distributions

anchor.snippet: its finite-dimensional distribution

annotations.note: Finite-dimensional distributions are `P.map (fun ω ↦ I.restrict (X · ω))` and form a projective measure family by `isProjectiveMeasureFamily_map_restrict`.

annotations.label: Consistency conditions for finite-dim distributions

anchor.snippet: The finite-dimensional distributions of a stochastic process satisfy two mathematical conditions known as consistency conditions

annotations.note: The Kolmogorov consistency conditions are precisely the `IsProjectiveMeasureFamily` property proven by `isProjectiveMeasureFamily_map_restrict`.

annotations.label: Stationarity (strict)

anchor.snippet: Stationarity is a mathematical property that a stochastic process has when all the random variables of that stochastic process are identically distributed

annotations.note: Grep for `Stationary`/`IsStationary` finds no probability-theoretic stationarity definition for processes.

annotations.label: Wide-sense stationarity

anchor.snippet: is said to be stationary in the wide sense

annotations.note: No wide-sense stationarity definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Filtration

anchor.snippet: A filtration is an increasing sequence of sigma-algebras

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.Filtration ι m` is defined as a monotone sequence of sub-σ-algebras.

annotations.label: Modification / version

anchor.snippet: A modification of a stochastic process is another stochastic process

annotations.note: Modifications are not defined as a named predicate; they appear inlined as `∀ t, X t =ᵐ[P] Y t` (e.g. in `IsAEKolmogorovProcess` and `map_restrict_eq_of_forall_ae_eq`).

annotations.label: Kolmogorov continuity theorem

anchor.snippet: the Kolmogorov continuity theorem says that there exists a modification of this process that has continuous sample paths with probability one

annotations.note: The hypothesis `IsKolmogorovProcess` is formalized, but the Kolmogorov–Chentsov conclusion (existence of a continuous modification) is not yet present.

annotations.label: Indistinguishable processes

anchor.snippet: are said be indistinguishable

annotations.note: No `Indistinguishable` predicate for processes exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Modifications + a.s. continuous ⇒ indistinguishable

anchor.snippet: are modifications of each other and are almost surely continuous

annotations.note: Statement absent since `Indistinguishable` is not defined.

annotations.label: Separable stochastic process

anchor.snippet: Separability is a property of a stochastic process based on its index set in relation to the probability measure

annotations.note: No notion of a separable stochastic process exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Doob's separability theorem

anchor.snippet: sometimes known as Doob’s separability theorem, says that any real-valued continuous-time stochastic process has a separable modification

annotations.note: Doob's separability theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Independence of stochastic processes

anchor.snippet: are said be independent

annotations.note: Independence of stochastic processes is captured by `IndepFun.process_indepFun_process` (and the `iIndepFun.iIndepFun_process` family) in `Probability.Independence.Process.Basic`.

annotations.label: Uncorrelated stochastic processes

anchor.snippet: are called uncorrelated if their cross-covariance

annotations.note: No `Uncorrelated`/cross-covariance-zero predicate for processes exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Independence implies uncorrelatedness

anchor.snippet: then they are also uncorrelated

annotations.note: Statement absent since `Uncorrelated` for processes is not defined.

annotations.label: Orthogonal stochastic processes

anchor.snippet: are called orthogonal if their cross-correlation

annotations.note: No orthogonal-stochastic-process predicate or cross-correlation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Skorokhod space

anchor.snippet: A Skorokhod space , also written as Skorohod space , is a mathematical space of all the functions that are right-continuous with left limits

annotations.note: Grep for `Skorokhod`/`Skorohod` returns no hits.

annotations.label: Markov process / Markov property

anchor.snippet: that have the Markov property, which means the next value of the Markov process depends on the current value, but it is conditionally independent of the previous values

annotations.note: Only `IsMarkovKernel` exists; the Markov property of a process is not defined.

annotations.label: Brownian motion and Poisson as Markov processes

anchor.snippet: are both examples of Markov processes

annotations.note: Neither Brownian motion nor Poisson processes are formalized, so this example is unavailable.

annotations.label: Markov chain

anchor.snippet: A Markov chain is a type of Markov process that has either discrete state space or discrete index set

annotations.note: No `MarkovChain` declaration; only Markov kernels appear in `Probability.Kernel`.

annotations.label: Markov random field

anchor.snippet: which results in collections of random variables known as Markov random fields

annotations.note: No Markov-random-field declaration exists.

annotations.label: Martingale

anchor.snippet: A martingale is a discrete-time or continuous-time stochastic process with the property that, at every instant, given the current value and all the past values of the process, the conditional expectation of every future value is equal to the current value

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.Martingale f ℱ μ` formalizes the conditional-expectation definition for adapted processes.

annotations.label: Symmetric random walk and Wiener process as martingales

anchor.snippet: are both examples of martingales, respectively, in discrete and continuous time

annotations.note: Neither symmetric random walk nor Wiener process is formalized, so this example is unavailable.

annotations.label: Partial sums martingale

anchor.snippet: the stochastic process formed from the successive partial sums

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks an explicit `partialSumsMartingale` construction (no such declaration found in `Probability.Martingale`).

annotations.label: Compensated Poisson process

anchor.snippet: resulting in a martingale called the compensated Poisson process

annotations.note: Without a Poisson process there is no compensated Poisson process.

annotations.label: Martingale convergence theorems

anchor.snippet: Martingales will converge, given some conditions on their moments

annotations.note: Doob's a.s. and L¹ martingale convergence are formalized via `Submartingale.ae_tendsto_limitProcess` and `Submartingale.tendsto_eLpNorm_one_limitProcess`.

annotations.label: Lévy process

anchor.snippet: a stochastic process

annotations.note: No `IsLevyProcess` declaration; `HasIndepIncrements` formalizes one of its defining properties (independent increments).

annotations.label: Random field (collection indexed by Euclidean space)

anchor.snippet: A random field is a collection of random variables indexed by a

annotations.note: No `RandomField` declaration exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Point process

anchor.snippet: A point process is a collection of points randomly located on some mathematical space

annotations.note: No `PointProcess` declaration exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kolmogorov's existence theorem

anchor.snippet: using Kolmogorov's existence theorem

annotations.note: Kolmogorov's extension theorem is realized via `isProjectiveLimit_infinitePi`, constructing the projective-limit measure for product spaces (also see `isProjectiveLimit_infinitePiNat`).

annotations.label: Left/right-continuous modifications of Poisson process

anchor.snippet: both the left-continuous modification and the right-continuous modification of a Poisson process have the same finite-dimensional distributions

annotations.note: No Poisson-process formalization, so its modifications are not available.

annotations.label: Black-Scholes model

anchor.snippet: the Black-Scholes model for option pricing

annotations.note: Grep finds no Black–Scholes formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Heston stochastic volatility model

anchor.snippet: The Heston model

annotations.note: No Heston/stochastic-volatility model is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Birth-death process for population dynamics

anchor.snippet: The birth-death process

annotations.note: No birth–death-process declaration found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Branching process

anchor.snippet: Another example is the branching process

annotations.note: No branching-process declaration found in Mathlib.
